(source: most recent 10-K and 10-Q from SEC)

You can see from here where Limoneira has been headed in the wrong direction, not just in Q2, but struggling for the past year and a half. They’re not really profitable right now, and that’s going to continue through this year if estimates to be believed, with non-GAAP EPS estimates of a 24¢ gain in Q3 and a 9¢ loss in Q4.

Revenue from their lemon business was down year over year in Q2, and where they’d previously offered a guidance of 5-5.5 million cartons this year, and now it’s down to 4.5-5 million cartons.

Avocados saw decreased production in Q2 year over year too, down from 1.6 million pounds in 2024 to 1.2 million pounds in 2025. That’s not a shock though, it’s reflective of biennial bearing of avocado trees, where they have a big crop one year and a small crop the next.

It came at a bad time though, because in 2024, they were only getting paid $1.47 per pound, and in 2025 they’ve been getting paid $2.26. The higher prices are a result of, among other things, the tariffs making imported avocados from Mexico more expensive. It was a particularly big deal around the Super Bowl, when avocado consumption in the US is highest.

Unfortunately, while the highest prices this year helped Limoneira increase its avocado revenue despite lower production, it happened in one of their off years for growing, and there’s no guarantee prices will remain high next year when their trees are producing more. Though tariffs probably will still be a thing, there is also the possibility that persistent higher prices will force people toward different snacks, ultimately lowering demand and normalizing prices.

Persistent Production Challenge: Growth Takes A While

While 2025 is likely to be a challenging year, it’s not as though Limoneira can expect a snap back year in 2026, either. More of their avocado trees will be in the high-yield year, so that segment should do better assuming prices are still strong, but their guidance for 2026 lemon production is even lower than the reduced guidance for 2025, down to 4-4.5 million cartons according to the conference call.

They’re also returning to a partnership in sales and marketing with Sunkist for citrus, which we’ll talk about later, and Limoneira says they expect it to improve margins, but reduce top-line revenue. If the avocado sector doesn’t strongly make up for this reduced production, 2026 might not be a lot better, and the EPS estimate for 2026 is still only 32¢, which is still nothing exciting at Limoneira’s current stock price. Even the ambitious 2027 estimate of 71¢ per share is just okay, and that undercuts the idea it would be a good contrarian pick, as I first thought.

Growth is the key here, but there’s an obvious problem: growth doesn’t just grow on trees, or rather the issue is it does just grow on trees. Limoneira is looking to grow their avocado production substantially, but per the conference call those trees aren’t going to be mature enough until the end of 2027. That means we won’t be seeing the results of those efforts for years.

The Return to Sunkist

Then we come to Sunkist. Limoneira had a long, long history of working with Sunkist in the citrus business, but in 2010 they ended that partnership, saying they would market their own lemons, and with multiple lemon brands they owned outright, they were highly confident.

Limoneira didn’t fall flat on their face with that, but here we are, 15 years later, and they announced earlier this week that they were re-merging their citrus sale and marketing operations with Sunkist. Though we don’t have all the details of this deal, it has the appearance of a quo ante bellum deal – that is they’re going right back to where they were in 2009.

In the conference call they conceded that top-line revenue would be down because of the merger, but predicted saving some $5 million in cost savings. It’s not clear what that would translate to on the bottom line, given the lower revenue.

While they insist in the call that this merger allows them to meet “several key objectives” and insisted “we’re not changing direction,” it certainly gives the appearance that they very much are changing direction.

We’ll have to wait to see what happens here, but I believe it’s fairly obviously them rethinking their decision to try to go it alone on lemons after trying it in 2010. They also told a story about Oxnard Lemon in the conference call which tried to justify the move, but I think raises more questions than it answers.

Limoneira acquired Oxnard Lemon, a full-line lemon packer, in 2018. But then they divested the wash and storage capacity, something they described themselves as being “very fortunate” to be able to do. After that, they ended up having to lease back that capacity at a much higher than expected price. It frankly doesn’t sound fortunate to me the way they tell it.

The argument they’re making is that with access to Sunkist’s infrastructure they won’t have that kind of problem in the future, but it feels like this was largely a self-created problem that they’re trying to solve by reversing another of the actions they had second thoughts about, splitting up their partnership with Sunkist. It makes me question the decision-making that underpins all of this.

No Rush to Buy Here

In the end, Limoneira does have some value locked away here, primarily in their real estate holdings and their efforts to grow avocado production. I still would be in no rush here, so I’m rating them a hold.

Limoneira’s effort to grow avocado production is going to take years to fully realize, the real estate profit is possible, but the timing there is uncertain. As such, even though the stock is around its 52-week low, I don’t see any obvious catalysts in 2025 or 2026. This is a long-term play, and prices might get lower still between now and when those avocado trees start maturing.

Investors should keep a close eye on that Sunkist merger, because that’s the more immediate matter. The $5 million cost savings will be welcome surely, but depending on how much it drags revenue down it might not result in substantial, meaningful GAAP earnings. With a 7.5¢ quarterly dividend, they too end up with a lower yield than the sector median, so even for the buy and hold crowd, there are likely better options out there at these prices.

data.title: PNC Financial: Long-Term Investors Should Wait For A Pullback
data.description:
PNC Financial (NYSE:PNC) is one of the largest banks in the U.S. and wants to expand its presence in the industry over the coming years, but growth prospects in the near term seem to be somewhat low and its sizable exposure to CRE is concerning, leading to a fair valuation right now. As this is my first coverage of PNC Financial, in this article I do an overview of its business profile and then review its most recent financial performance, to see if it offers value or not for long-term investors within the banking sector.

Business Overview

PNC Financial is a regional bank headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, being one of the largest banking groups in the U.S. Indeed, at the end of last year, its total assets amounted to about $560 billion, being among the largest banks in the U.S. by this measure, even though it’s much smaller than large U.S. banks like Wells Fargo (WFC) or Citigroup (C) which have total assets close or above $2 trillion. Having said that, PNC Financial is among the largest regional banks in the country, having a similar size to Truist Financial (TFC) and slightly smaller than compared to US Bancorp (USB). Its current market value is about $70 billion, and its shares trade on the New York Stock Exchange.

Its business model is diversified across retail banking, corporate and investment banking (CIB), and asset management. This is a distinctive factor compared to other regional banks, which usually are much more concentrated on retail and commercial banking, while PNC Financial has a better business diversification. However, its asset management unit has a small weight on the group, making retail and CIB the two most important segments.

Geographically, the bank has been expanding its presence throughout the country over the past few years, and is nowadays present in the most important markets, both through a physical branch presence and a digital retail offering. Its growth strategy is to continue to expand its physical branch presence over the coming years, aiming to invest about $1.5 billion over the next five years, by opening some 200 new branches and renovating other 1,400 branches.

The bank believes that building scale is essential to have a sustainable business model in the banking industry, a trend that was accelerated since 2023 following the regional banking turmoil. Indeed, customers and businesses started to question the sustainability of smaller banks following the collapse of SVB Financial in March 2023, preferring to do business and take their deposits with larger banks. While PNC Financial benefited from this trend due to its relatively large size among the regional banking space, the U.S. large banks benefited even more, showing that size and scale are important factors in the industry. Therefore, PNC Financial’s strategy is to continue to gain scale in the U.S. banking sector and has also increased marketing spending to create brand awareness nationally.

This means PNC Financial should be able to grow its business by expanding its presence nationwide and also by gaining market share, especially against smaller competitors, plus it may also pursue acquisitions if they make sense financially and from a business strategy standpoint.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, PNC Financial has delivered a relatively stable performance in recent years, as the tailwind of higher rates was offset to some extent by higher deposit costs following the regional banking turmoil in 2023. Despite that, its deposit base has been quite stable following some outflows after Q1 2023, showing that PNC Financial enjoys a good reputation in the banking sector, which is critical for the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

As shown in the previous graph, its total deposits have been quite stable at around $420 billion over the last year, while the average cost of deposits has been on a declining trend following the Fed’s rate cuts initiated last September. According to the bank, its cumulative deposits beta is 51%, showing that PNC Financial’s deposit sensitivity to rates is not great, boding well for its margins.

On the loans side, PNC Financial’s loan book amounted to $317 billion at the end of last quarter, which means its loan-to-deposits (L/D) ratio was 75% in Q1 2025. This is a good level in the banking sector and slightly below the regional bank average that is around 80%, showing that PNC Financial’s business is mainly funded through customer deposits and the bank has low need to finance itself on the wholesale market. This leads to a more sustainable business model over the long term, being a positive feature of its business profile.

As usual in the regional banking sector, PNC Financial is a bank that is significantly exposed to rates, which can be a double-edge sword depending on the rate environment. In the last quarter, net interest income (NII) amounted to nearly $3.5 billion, while non-interest income was above $1.8 billion. Therefore, total revenues were close to $5.45 billion, of which about 64% were generated from NII. This is an acceptable level in the banking sector and lower than compared to other banks that are much more exposed to NII, like Comerica (CMA) or Zions (ZION), being a positive factor on a relative basis during a potential period of lower rates ahead.

Moreover, PNC Financial has shown less sensitivity to rates than it could be expected from a bank that is highly exposed to retail and CIB. Its NII only declined by 1% on a quarterly basis in Q1 2025 and its net interest margin increased by three basis points (bps) to 2.78%, which can be considered a resilient performance. Other revenues had a worse performance, as non-interest revenues declined by 2% on a quarterly basis, explained by lower capital markets and advisory revenues which were impacted by higher market volatility.

Regarding costs, the bank had a good performance considering that operating expenses amounted to less than $3.4 billion in Q1 2025, representing a decline of 3% from the previous quarter. Its efficiency ratio was 62% in the last quarter, an acceptable level compared to its peers, even though this is a level that is not among the most efficient banks in the U.S. and shows that PNC Financial still has some work to do to improve its business efficiency over the coming years.

On the asset quality front, like many regional banks, PNC Financial has a significant exposure to Commercial Real Estate (CRE) even though the bank has been reducing its exposure to CRE in recent quarters, to reduce risk in its balance sheet. Nevertheless, at the end of last quarter, CRE accounted for about 10% of its loan book or about $32 billion, which is a sizable exposure. The most important segments of CRE are multifamily and office loans, which together account for 7% of its total loan book, while other segments like industrial, warehouses, or retail have smaller weights on loans.

The segment that is more problematic is office, but PNC Financial has been reducing exposure to office loans and has increased reserves for loan losses over the past few quarters, being now on a more comfortable position than compared to some quarters ago. Reflecting its more adequate level of reserves related to office, net charge-offs in the office segment declined to only $8 million in Q1 2025 (vs. $62 million in the previous quarter), showing that asset quality in the office segment has stabilized and further losses may be much more limited ahead.

Despite its exposure to CRE and office specifically, PNC Financial’s net charge-off ratio was only 26 bps in Q1 2025, which is a very acceptable ratio compared to peers, showing that the bank’s overall credit quality is good and beyond CRE its loan book seems to be sound.

Its net income in Q1 2025 amounted to $1.4 billion, representing a quarterly decline of 7% and its return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability in the banking sector, was 11.6%. This is an acceptable level of profitability compared to other regional banks but is not among the most profitable banks in the U.S., showing that PNC Financial has further room to improve its profitability by growing its business and achieving higher operating efficiency over the next few years.

Regarding capital, its CET1 ratio was 10.6% at the end of last March, being above its regulatory requirement, but below some of its peers. I don’t see this ratio has being particularly strong, even though it has increased over the past few quarters through retained earnings and is now at a more acceptable level (it was around 10% at the beginning of 2024 and around 9.5% by the middle of 2023).

Despite this relatively weaker capital position than other banks, PNC Financial has returned a good part of earnings to shareholders through dividend and share buybacks, a strategy that is expected to be maintained ahead. During the first quarter of 2025, the bank returned about $800 million to shareholders, representing around 57% of its quarterly earnings. Regarding its dividend, the bank is currently distributing $1.60 per quarter, or $6.40 annually, while current consensus estimates are for the bank to raise its annual dividend to about $6.55 per share related to 2025 earnings. This shows that PNC Financial’s dividend growth prospects are somewhat low ahead. On the other hand, its current dividend yield is about 3.6%, being slightly above the banking sector average even though it’s not a high-dividend yield.

Regarding its valuation, PNC Financial is currently trading at about 1.39x book value, which seems to be a fair multiple for a stable bank like PNC Financial. As can be seen in the next graph, its valuation multiple has traded between 1.1-1.7x book value over the past few years, being nowadays at the middle of its valuation range.

Assuming that interest rates are more likely to go down in the future and macroeconomic uncertainty has increased over the past few weeks due to Trump’s trade war, I think a higher valuation multiple is not much likely in the short term, making PNC Financial’s valuation fair right now.

Conclusion

PNC Financial is a regional bank with an interesting profile due to a higher diversification both geographically and by operating segments than other regional banks, but it has relatively muted growth prospects and sizable exposure to CRE, two factors that don’t bode well for higher profitability ahead. Therefore, I see its current valuation as being fair, and I think investors should wait for a pullback to potentially consider its shares in the future.

data.title: Dividend Investors: Understand Your Risks
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Today I want to talk about "risk". There is a sense out there that "high dividends = high risk" and that often leads investors to believe that they are reducing risk by investing in lower-yielding investments. Is this true?

As with most pieces of bumper sticker wisdom, there is an element of truth to it. Yields get higher as prices are lower, and an increasing risk is one of many reasons why the price of an investment might be lower. If you were holding something that was yielding 5%, and now it's yielding 10%, the odds are high that the risk increased. Essentially, the price of the investment decreased by 50%, and when prices move that significantly, there is likely a reason for it, and that reason is likely that the market believes the investment is now considered higher risk than it was previously. The market isn't always right, but sometimes it is. This is why you can't simply enter the market blindly and buy high-yield investments. You must examine each situation and make your best effort to predict the future outcome of the investment.

On the other hand, if you are buying a company that is already yielding 10%, you're taking a lot less risk than the person who bought that same company at 5%. To be precise, you are buying the same number of shares at half the price, so you are putting half of the capital at risk for the same result, or you are putting the same amount of capital at risk for twice the potential reward. Paying less is lower risk. Collecting more capital back sooner is a lower risk.

Yet, investors often pay high prices for low-yielding investments, believing that they are reducing their risk and improving their future total returns. Are they really?

Today, we are going to imagine that the year is 2007 and two people have accumulated $500,000 and are heading into retirement. Let's meet them.

Income Irene is heading into retirement and is following the Income Method. She has a $500,000 nest egg, and with Social Security, she calculates that she needs to withdraw $30,000 per year to maintain her lifestyle. Yes, she heard that you are only supposed to withdraw 4%, but she isn't really ready to give up her house to downsize. Besides, she is able to construct a portfolio that is expected to produce a yield of 8.6% this year. Irene invests her money in a high-yielding portfolio of stocks similar to what we do at HDO (High Dividend Opportunities).

Cautious Cal is also heading to retirement. Unfortunately, Cal was laid off. He had hoped to grow his portfolio to $750,000 so that he could follow the 4% rule to get the $30,000/year income that he needs. When he was laid off, he had $500,000 in his retirement account. Cautious Cal has heard that high yield is high risk. So, even though he could really use the excess cash, he decided to invest solely in Dividend Aristocrats. These are companies that have raised their dividend every year for 25+ years, even through the Dot-com bust. In fact, the charts for his holdings are fantastic. Over the past 10 years, the Dividend Aristocrat portfolio has had a CAGR of 13.85%, while the S&P 500 has only produced 8.34%. So, while Cautious Cal would have preferred to work for a few more years, he's making the best of it and investing in what he believes are low-risk investments. He buys 47 of the original 57 dividend aristocrat stocks.

Which retiree do you believe is taking less risk?

Selection Methodology:

Team HDO: The Team HDO portfolio was selected from currently held common equity holdings in the HDO Model Portfolio, with the only requirement being that it had to be trading as of January 1st, 2007. It represents a good representation of the Model portfolio with BDCs, REITs, CEFs, MLPs, utilities, and other high-yielding investments. Note that it does include numerous holdings that have cut their dividends over the years, like Annaly Capital (NLY). In fact, by our count, only four of these holdings went from 2007 to 2025 without cutting their dividends. Some are currently underperforming, including holdings such as Macerich (MAC) and Medical Properties Trust (MPW). We made no attempt to hide poor picks or emphasize our best picks. The only litmus test was that the stock was publicly traded as of January 1, 2007. The full list of stocks included in this analysis is available to members and includes 19 holdings that were publicly traded in 2007 and are publicly traded now.

Dividend Aristocrats: We went through the original Dividend Aristocrats list as of 2005. We included every company that was on that list, except those that have gone out of business or are no longer publicly traded.

Total Return

First, here is a look at the portfolios' performance, as conventionally presented: Total Return, measured by the stocks assuming full reinvestment of dividends.

By this measure, Income Irene came up a little short with $2.46 million compared to Cautious Cal's $2.72 million.

That is what people often look at and demand that I admit high yield is high risk. Here is what I look at:

In the year 2024, Income Irene's portfolio produced $180,774 in dividends compared to Cautious Cal at $68,044. Irene is receiving 2.65x more income than Cal. This is actually very close to 2007 when Income Irene's portfolio produced 2.75 times the income that Cautious Cal's portfolio did. Despite 17 years for the Dividend Aristocrats to do their thing, and despite 79% of Irene's holdings cutting dividends at some point, Irene's portfolio maintained its lead in income generation while posting a competitive total return.

But wait, Irene and Cal are both retired. Both of them were withdrawing funds from their portfolio. People love to look at total returns, but total returns don't represent reality unless you are selling your entire portfolio today. In the real world, retirees aren't selling everything today. They are withdrawing a little bit every year to live off of. What is the point of a retirement portfolio if you aren't going to use it to retire?

This is where Portfolio Visualizer is one of the best tools on the Internet to illustrate real retiree behavior, not fantasy portfolios where money is never withdrawn.

High-Yield Outperforms in Real Conditions

Here we assume that both retirees withdrew $30,000 in year one, and they increased their withdrawals with inflation every year thereafter. Why $30k? First, it accounts for approximately 75% of the high-yield portfolio's income, which follows the Income Method. Second, it's a realistic amount for a retiree to want to withdraw.

For a retiree who accumulated $500,000 during their working years, $30,000 per year is a great supplement to their Social Security, helping to fill the gap between what they were earning during their working years. It isn't a pie in the sky, living on a yacht, and partying with rockstars number. It's a reasonable supplement that, for the average retiree, is the difference between scraping by and worrying about the bills and living a reasonably comfortable lifestyle.

When we add withdrawals, with the same stocks in both portfolios and the same time frame, the numbers change significantly.

Suddenly, Team HDO outperforms. Income Irene has an ending portfolio value of $815,704, while Cautious Cal has $790,603.

On the income side, Irene's portfolio produced about $60,000 in 2024, while Cautious Cal's portfolio produced about $20,000.

Adjusted for inflation, the withdrawal was slightly under $47,000. In other words, Income Irene's portfolio is still producing significantly more than she is withdrawing, while Cautious Cal's dividends have always been below his withdrawals. Cal sold $27,000 worth of shares last year to fund his lifestyle. Irene reinvested over $13,000 into new investments.

So, who is taking more risk?

Dividend Cuts

Investors live in fear of dividend cuts. I get it; they aren't fun, and it's definitely nice to avoid them when you can. However, the Team HDO portfolio above had many holdings that cut their dividend over the years. In fact, nearly 80% of the holdings cut their dividend during this investment period. Yet, despite those cuts, Irene's income grew from $40,000 in 2007 to $60,000 in 2024 – a 50% increase!

Her income wasn't immune to volatility. It declined by approximately 25% during the GFC. It declined by approximately 10% during the COVID-19 period. Income Irene did nothing to try to avoid these cuts; she just held through, and in the long run, her income grew 50% even as she withdrew money every year.

Cal thought he was investing more cautiously. However, the GFC was rough on him. His income went from about $15,000 to below $10,000 – a 34% reduction. Keep in mind that his yield in 2007 was 3%. By 2024, Cal's dividend income increased by just 33%.

Irene had more dividend cuts and fewer dividend raises. Yet, the largest reduction in her income was smaller, and Irene's portfolio, in the heart of the GFC, produced more income than Cal's portfolio produced in any year. Over 18 years, the growth of the Dividend Aristocrats couldn't catch up.

Is there a higher risk of a dividend cut among high-yielders? Maybe. Roughly half of the Dividend Aristocrats never cut their dividend, while about 80% of the high-yielders did. However, when an investment yields only 3%, and it cuts, that's a significant deal. However, when an investment yields 8-10%, and it cuts 50%, it still has a higher yield than something that yields 3%.

This is what happened in her portfolio. Yes, she saw cuts, but despite the cuts, she has excess income to reinvest and buy more shares. As the companies resolved their issues, most of them raised their dividends, and Irene owned more shares.

Cal, on the other hand, found himself selling shares every single year. The dividend growth in his portfolio needed to be higher than the dividends lost from the shares he sold. And in many years, it did, but in many, it didn't.

Volatility

Another measure of risk is price volatility. Irene's portfolio did see larger price swings. The largest sell-off was -53.78% compared to Cautious Cal's -47.05%.

We would suggest that psychologically, there isn't a big difference between -54% and -47%. Psychologically, when going through that big drawdown in 2009, Irene was collecting approximately $30,000 in dividends, while Cal was collecting just $10,000 in dividends. The withdrawal plan called for withdrawing $32,000 that year, so Irene had to choose between selling $2,000 worth of stock at unattractive prices or just cutting back spending by $2,000. Cal was faced with selling $22,000 in stock at unattractive prices. Who is more stressed?

But, by the numbers, it is true that the higher-yield stocks were more volatile in price.

Conclusion: The High Risk of Assuming You Can Avoid Risk

Often, I see investors talking about "capital preservation" or avoiding risk. The stock market is not a good place for either one. Yes, some stocks might be less volatile than others, but "less volatile" is very different from "no volatility". In my experience, I see people like Cautious Cal panic. They saw their portfolio value crash 40% when they invested in stocks that they believed were very low risk. He invested in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats – there is no list of stocks that are perceived as lower risk. From a company standpoint, it is a list of very strong companies with excellent historical track records. Their track records are great until they aren't, and they become "fallen angels".

The harsh reality of the stock market is that stock prices are volatile. Prices will swing, and declines of +50% are not unusual. In terms of having the smallest maximum drawdown among all the stocks in both portfolios, it was McDonald's (MCD) at -23%. Who says fast food is bad for your health? But both portfolios have numerous holdings that pulled back over 50%.

Common equities are publicly traded, and public markets are subject to the whims of our neighbors. When our neighbors are fearful, they sell. When prices go down, they become more fearful and they sell more. The herd mentality is strong in the market.

Investing in large companies doesn't protect you from price swings.

Does investing in lower-yielding stocks protect you from dividend cuts? Well, yes and no. It is probably fair to say that higher-yielding investments are going to see more variation in the dividends they pay. That means cuts will be more frequent. Whereas, if you buy a dividend aristocrat, the risks of a cut for any particular company are somewhat lower. However, the impact of a cut is much higher for a dividend aristocrat.

In the high-yield world, we are often talking about RICs (Regulated Investment Companies). This is a corporate structure that means the company won't pay taxes at the corporate level if it distributes substantially all of its taxable income. As a result, these companies are paying out nearly 100% of their taxable income and sometimes more. When taxable income declines, the dividend tends to follow. Of course, taxable income goes up, too, and when it does, the dividend follows.

Whereas a dividend aristocrat might be paying out 30-40% of its taxable income. It has a wider buffer, so when earnings decline, it can just pay an elevated payout for a while. It means fewer cuts, but when they happen, they are brutal. An already low yield gets lower or disappears, and then the share price crashes because the only reason the Cautious Cal's of the world bought something yielding 3% was because they believed the dividend would grow for decades.

So, while buying Dividend Aristocrats reduces the frequency you might experience dividend cuts, the severity of the cuts is much higher. The reason is that when you invest at 3%, you need the company to increase its dividend 10-15%/year to make the investment worthwhile. Even just pausing growth is a huge negative. When you invest at 8%+ you don't need the company to increase the dividend at all. More importantly, you have a much larger cushion in your income to absorb cuts. If you're Cautious Cal, every dividend cut means you have to sell more shares during a bad time to meet your cash needs. If you're Income Irene, your portfolio can absorb a GFC – the worst dividend-cutting period in history – and still produce the cash you need.

It would be a wonderful world if we could avoid every dividend cut and only invest in assets that produce double-digit returns. But we don't live or invest in the land of rainbows and unicorns. We live in the real world, where some investments we make are terrible and many are mediocre. Every time we invest in common equity, we are taking a risk. There is a risk that the price will fall, and a risk that dividends may be cut. We can't predict the future, and neither can the CEOs of the companies we invest in. Mistakes will be made, sometimes by us, sometimes by the companies we invest in.

At the end of the day, the lowest risk is the cash you have in hand right now. The dividends you receive this month lower your risk. It's cash in hand that means you don't have to sell shares to fund your lifestyle. In their efforts to "reduce risk", the Cautious Cals of the world take on one of the highest risks in the market. They rely on selling shares to get the income they need.

Income Irene went through the GFC, and she went through COVID. She saw the prices in her portfolio fluctuate, and she noticed several dividend cuts. Yet, despite all those risks being realized through what were two of the toughest periods for income stocks, her income comfortably covered the amount she wanted to withdraw. She wasn't forced to sell at poor prices. Instead, she was buying while prices were low. If another GFC starts tomorrow, Income Irene won't need to sell a share. Cautious Cal will have to sell more shares if the market is bullish, bearish, or somewhere in between.

It's budgeting 101: Spend less than your income. Spending more than your income is a very high risk. Yet, many plan to do so and think that their choice is somehow "conservative."

I'll invest in high-yield without blinking an eye. Spending more than my income is a risk I would never take.

Income Irene is only spending the money she has collected in dividends. Cautious Cal is selling shares at market prices to fund his lifestyle. Who do you think is taking a higher risk?
